title: Accenture booked $5.9B doing what a solo operator sells for $2K
hook_line: Last year, Accenture booked five point nine billion dollars of generative AI work. Not building AI — installing it.
cliffhanger_line: And the same service, at solo scale, is a two thousand dollar project. Who's actually buying it — that's the part nobody explains.
pinned_comment: The full breakdown — who pays, the exact stack, the honest math: [long-form link]
why: The channel's core arbitrage stated in 15 seconds; number-first hook; resolves nothing about how

title: Accenture's only growing business line
hook_line: Accenture's overall bookings were flat last year. One line doubled.
cliffhanger_line: By late twenty twenty-five they stopped reporting AI separately — because it touched eighty percent of large deals. The bottom of this market looks nothing like the top.
pinned_comment: What the bottom of the market pays a solo operator — full breakdown: [long-form link]
why: Sourced enterprise evidence with a curiosity gap pointing at the solo version

title: The $40K/month claim, fact-checked
hook_line: There's a number making the rounds: a solo AI agency doing forty thousand dollars a month.
cliffhanger_line: That number comes from a creator-economy blog, not audited books. The documented floor is more interesting — and more believable.
pinned_comment: The believable numbers, with sources — full breakdown: [long-form link]
why: Trust-building skeptic short; models the channel's fact-checking voice; withholds the floor number

title: Your cost of goods on a $2,000 project rounds to zero
hook_line: The whole stack for this business costs less than a hundred dollars a month.
cliffhanger_line: Which means the entire game is finding the first client — and that's where most people get it exactly backwards.
pinned_comment: How the first client actually happens — full breakdown: [long-form link]
why: Uses the stack section's own micro-loop as the cliffhanger into the playbook
